entries.why: ChatGPT web-search leaves a 'contentReference[oaicite:N]{index=N}' citation stub in its answer; if it survives into pasted text it is near-proof the passage was copied straight out of ChatGPT unedited.
Internal source IDs (turn0search1, turn0news5) that ChatGPT's browsing tool emits around citations. They are meaningless to a reader and only appear when raw browsing output is pasted verbatim.
entries.example: according to recent reports turn0search2 the figure is higher

entries.why: The model narrating its own training boundary ('as of my last knowledge update', 'my training data goes up to ...'). A human author has no knowledge cutoff; this is an unedited assistant voice leaking through.
entries.example: As of my last knowledge update in early 2023, the policy had not changed.

entries.why: Assistant hedge about lacking live data or web access. Never something a human writer says about their own article; a direct leak of the chatbot persona.
entries.example: I don't have access to real-time data, but as of my last update...

entries.why: Self-identification as an LLM. Alongside 'As an AI language model', the single most unambiguous tell of unedited chatbot output.
entries.example: As a large language model, I can help you draft this.

entries.term: Here is the revised / rewritten version
entries.why: Editing-handoff preamble the model prepends when returning a rewrite. If it appears in a finished piece, the author pasted the assistant's wrapper along with the content.
entries.example: Here is the revised version of your introduction:

entries.pattern: ^\s{0,3}#{0,4}\s*(?:Future Outlook|Challenges and Legacy|Key Takeaways|Final Thoughts|In Summary|Conclusion and Next Steps|The Road Ahead)\s*:?\s*$
entries.why: Wikipedia documents these boilerplate section titles as an AI structural tell; the model resolves any topic into the same optimistic outline scaffolding.

Wikipedia's 'present participle endings' tell: the real clause is followed by a floating '-ing' summary that editorializes its own significance ('..., underscoring the importance of ...'). A hallmark of generated wrap-up prose.
entries.example: The bridge opened in 1932, cementing the city as a regional hub.

entries.why: Wikipedia-listed puffery closer: something 'leaves an indelible mark' on something else. Vague significance-assertion with no concrete detail.
entries.example: Her work left an indelible mark on the genre.

entries.why: Decorative prestige-metaphor collocation ('a beacon of hope/innovation'). Near-poetic register that reads as generated filler in neutral prose.
entries.example: The clinic became a beacon of hope for the community.

entries.why: 2026 detector-blacklist intro verb ('let us demystify X'). A packaging word the model reaches for to frame explainer content.
entries.example: In this guide we demystify tax brackets.

entries.why: Corporate-hype collocation on every 2025-2026 AI-word blacklist; the model overstates ordinary change as a 'paradigm shift'.
entries.example: This represents a paradigm shift in how we work.
